item.title: Context Spacesuits in Agentic AI
item.description:
Every agentic AI system that operates in a repository, codebase, or organizational context must solve the same problem: before the model can do anything useful, it must be told what it’s looking at, what tools are available, what rules apply, and what happened before. This package — the context spacesuit — is loaded at session start and determines the agent’s perceivable world for the duration of the session.

How current systems implement it

Claude Code loads a layered suit: a system prompt (~3K tokens) defining role and tools, then CLAUDE.md files (project instructions, checked into the codebase), then rules (from .claude/rules/), then tool definitions, then session state including git status and recent context. Skills load on demand when invoked. The total can reach 25-30K tokens before any user input.

item.title: Programmable Interface Architectures — Five Systems Where Using IS Programming
item.description:
Programmable Interface Architectures

Five systems from five decades share a structural property: the boundary between using the system and programming the system does not exist. The interface IS the programming surface. This text surveys the specific mechanisms each system uses, because the mechanisms — not the philosophy — are what an implementation can learn from.

Plan 9 — Per-Process Namespaces and 9P

Plan 9 from Bell Labs (1992) eliminates the Unix global filesystem namespace. Each process has its own mutable namespace. When user A mounts a remote resource at /home/work, that mount exists only in processes spawned by A. User B’s processes see a different namespace.

item.title: Blockchain and Web3 Patterns for Autonomous Systems
item.description:
Blockchain systems have spent a decade solving problems that any autonomous system eventually faces: how do independent agents coordinate without a central authority? How do you track who created what, and who owns it now? How do you make rules self-enforcing? How do you motivate contribution when you cannot compel it?

Most of these solutions are over-engineered for our purposes — we do not need trustless consensus among adversaries, and we are not issuing tokens. But the patterns are portable. This text extracts the design lessons from DAOs, smart contracts, NFTs, web3 game economies, tokenomics, content-addressed storage, Merkle verification, and the MUD autonomous worlds framework, and maps each to concrete adoption recommendations for non-blockchain autonomous systems.

item.title: Dwarf Fortress Simulation as a Model for Agentic Systems
item.description: Dwarf Fortress (DF) is a colony simulation where the player manages a fortress of dwarves who act autonomously under indirect control. The player designates what should happen; dwarves decide how, when, and whether to do it based on their skills, personalities, and the state of the world. This is structurally identical to the problem of autonomous agent orchestration: a system of specialized workers, a queue of tasks, resource constraints, and emergent behavior from their interaction.

item.title: Guix and Guile as a Composition Layer for System Management
item.description: Most infrastructure as code tools use a domain-specific language to describe desired state. Terraform uses HCL. NixOS uses the Nix expression language. Ansible uses YAML with Jinja templates. GNU Guix takes a different approach: it uses Guile Scheme, a general-purpose programming language, as its configuration language. The entire system configuration is a Scheme value that can be passed to functions, transformed, and composed using the same mechanisms that any Scheme program uses.

item.title: MUD Framework and Autonomous Worlds: On-Chain ECS Architecture
item.description:
MUD Framework and Autonomous Worlds

Part One: What MUD Is

MUD is a framework for building autonomous worlds — fully on-chain applications on Ethereum and EVM chains where game state, logic, and data all persist on the blockchain itself. Built and maintained by Lattice, MUD provides tools, patterns, and infrastructure that make decentralized, composable game worlds practical to build.

Unlike traditional game architectures where a centralized server holds state and enforces rules, MUD worlds are governed by smart contracts. Every transaction modifies on-chain state. Every player interaction is a blockchain transaction. The world persists without any central authority — it is autonomous in the strictest sense.

item.title: RimWorld Colony Management as a Model for Agentic Systems
item.description:
RimWorld is a colony simulation where the player manages colonists indirectly — setting priorities, schedules, and research directions while colonists autonomously execute work. The player never directly controls a colonist’s hands. This constraint produces a management system that maps remarkably well onto multi-agent orchestration, where a harness directs autonomous agents without micromanaging their inference.

Work Priorities: The 1-4 Dispatch System

RimWorld offers two priority modes. In simple mode, each colonist toggles work types on or off. In manual mode, each work type gets a priority from 1 (highest) to 4 (lowest), or blank (disabled). A colonist completes all available priority-1 work before touching priority-2 work. Within the same priority level, work types listed further left take precedence.

item.title: Tech Messaging Systems as Models for Agentic Coordination
item.description:
For a distributed heterogeneous agentic system at nation-scale, message-passing is the primary concern: how do agents coordinate when they run in different processes, at different times, under different models, across different sites? Real-world messaging systems have solved slices of this problem. Each brings a pattern the 561 system can adopt.

Apache Kafka — log-based messaging

Kafka stores messages as an append-only log partitioned across brokers. Producers write to topics; consumers read by offset, replay at will. The log IS the state — downstream systems derive their view by consuming the log from offset 0. Retention is time-bounded (hours to forever). Ordering is guaranteed within a partition, not across.

item.title: The 561 System Architecture
item.description: The 561 system is a multi-agent knowledge management and autonomous orchestration system built as a git repository. It combines a structured library of grounded knowledge, a multi-agent operational harness, a Rust daemon with EAVT graph stores, and a mathematical framework that unifies the system’s ontology with its operational dynamics. The system is designed for the 561 Group, a confederacy working toward Black Liberation and Indigenous Sovereignty, and serves as that organization’s computer system.

item.title: Agentic AI in Industrial Control and Physical Infrastructure: A 2026 Survey
item.description:
AI agents are moving from software environments into the physical world. As of early 2026, agentic systems — AI that autonomously perceives, decides, and acts — are interfacing with manufacturing lines, power grids, building systems, farms, and homes. This survey maps what exists, how it works, and what safety mechanisms constrain it.

The motivation is practical: understanding the current state of AI-to-physical-system integration informs how any system intending to control physical infrastructure (printers, hydroponics, servers, networks) should be designed.

item.title: Agentic Harness Landscape, April 2026
item.description:
This is a survey of what exists in the space of agent harnesses, orchestration frameworks, memory systems, and skill evolution as of April 2026. The field is moving fast. This text captures the state of play across four categories: Claude Code harnesses specifically, general agentic frameworks, memory systems, and skill/learning architectures.

Claude Code Harnesses

Claude Code’s plugin architecture — skills, hooks, agents, commands, rules installed under .claude/ — has spawned a small ecosystem of third-party harnesses that customize the agent’s behavior without touching the model.

item.title: AI Memory Systems: MemGPT/Letta and Mem0
item.description:
The context window is finite. Everything interesting about AI memory follows from this constraint. Two systems — Letta (formerly MemGPT) and Mem0 — take fundamentally different approaches to working within it. Letta treats the context window as RAM and builds an operating system around it. Mem0 treats memories as facts and builds a knowledge graph. Both are instructive for anyone designing memory for agentic systems.

Letta: The Operating System Metaphor

Letta’s core insight is that context window management is memory management, and the best-understood framework for memory management is the operating system. The paper (Packer et al., 2023) made this explicit: the context window is main memory, and everything that doesn’t fit gets paged to external storage.

item.title: Aider Architecture Analysis
item.description:
Aider is an AI pair programming tool that operates from the terminal. It edits code in a git repository, auto-commits changes, and uses a “repo map” to give the LLM context about the full codebase without sending every file. This analysis covers the architectural decisions most relevant to agentic harness design: how Aider builds context, structures edits, routes between models, and verifies changes.

Repo Map: PageRank Over a Definition-Reference Graph

The repo map (repomap.py) is Aider’s most distinctive architectural feature. Rather than dumping file contents into context, it builds a ranked summary of the codebase’s structure — which definitions matter most given the current conversation.

item.title: Cline Architecture Analysis
item.description:
Cline is a VS Code extension that gives an AI agent access to IDE tools — file editing, terminal execution, browser automation, and MCP servers — through a structured task execution loop. This analysis reads the source code to extract architectural patterns relevant to our harness design.

Architecture Overview

Cline follows a three-layer hierarchy:

  1. WebviewProvider — manages VS Code webview lifecycle, HTML rendering, message passing
  2. Controller — single source of truth for persistent state, coordinates tasks, manages MCP connections
  3. Task — executes AI requests in a loop, handles tool approval, streams responses

The webview (React) communicates with the extension host through VS Code’s postMessage system, serialized via gRPC-like protobuf. State lives in VS Code’s global state, workspace state, and secrets storage.

item.title: Continuous Learning in Agentic Harnesses
item.description:
Agentic harness systems face a fundamental problem: LLM agents lose all behavioral context at session boundaries. Each new session starts from the same base prompt, ignorant of what worked or failed in prior sessions. Continuous learning is the set of mechanisms by which a harness captures experience during sessions and converts it into durable behavioral changes that persist across sessions — without retraining the underlying model.

This survey compares six systems that address this problem, ranging from production harnesses (Everything Claude Code, OpenClaw, Cline) to research frameworks (Voyager, Reflexion, LATS). The comparison reveals a shared architecture — observe, detect, persist, validate, promote — instantiated differently in each system.

item.title: ECC Agents and Commands: Structural Analysis and Comparison
item.description:
Everything Claude Code (ECC) is a community plugin providing 38 agents, 72 commands, 156 skills, and automated hooks for Claude Code. This analysis compares its architecture against the 561 Group’s agentic harness (27 agents, 80+ commands, 60+ skills) to identify patterns worth adopting, gaps to fill, and design differences that reflect genuinely different needs.

1. Agent Prompt Structure

ECC Pattern

ECC agents use a consistent four-field YAML frontmatter:

name: code-reviewer
description: Expert code review specialist...
tools: ["Read", "Grep", "Glob", "Bash"]
model: sonnet

The prompt body follows a standard structure: role statement, process/workflow (numbered steps), checklist tables, code examples showing BAD/GOOD patterns, and a success metrics section. ECC agents are self-contained instruction manuals: a senior developer could follow the prompt as a checklist without any agent infrastructure.

item.title: ECC Hook Architecture Analysis
item.description:
Everything Claude Code (ECC) is the most mature open-source Claude Code plugin, with 25+ hooks across 7 lifecycle events, a shared utility library, and a profile-gated execution framework. This analysis compares ECC’s hook architecture against the 561 harness to identify patterns worth adopting, hooks worth porting, and design decisions worth understanding.

Architecture Overview

ECC Hook Inventory

ECC registers hooks across every Claude Code lifecycle event:

Event Count Purpose
SessionStart 1 Project detection, skill listing, package manager detection
PreToolUse 12 Safety gates, quality checks, observation capture, governance
PreCompact 1 State preservation before context compaction
PostToolUse 10 Logging, quality gates, observation capture, governance
PostToolUseFailure 1 MCP health tracking
Stop 6 Batch formatting, console.log scanning, session persistence, cost tracking, desktop notifications
SessionEnd 1 Observer cleanup

The 561 harness registers hooks across the same events plus UserPromptSubmit (which ECC does not use), with 29 registered hooks total. Both systems have comparable breadth, but their depth differs in specific areas.

item.title: ECC Skill System Analysis
item.description:
Everything Claude Code (ECC) is a community-maintained Claude Code plugin containing 156 curated skills, plus infrastructure for generating, importing, and evolving skills at runtime. This text analyzes how ECC’s skill system works and extracts patterns relevant to the 561 harness.

What a Skill Is

A skill is a passive knowledge module that Claude Code loads based on context. ECC distinguishes skills from four sibling component types:

Component Activation Role
Skill Context-based (automatic) Knowledge repository
Agent Explicit delegation Task executor
Command User-invoked (/command) User action
Hook Event-triggered Automation
Rule Always active Constraint

The key distinction: skills are passive. They do not execute. They inject knowledge into context when Claude determines they are relevant. Rules are always loaded; skills are loaded on demand. This is the fundamental architectural decision — skills trade guaranteed presence for reduced token cost.

item.title: LPMud Architecture for Agentic Systems
item.description:
This text is a source-level analysis of two production LPMud codebases — FluffOS (the C++ driver) and the Discworld mudlib (the LPC world library) — read specifically for patterns applicable to the 561 agentic system. The mapping between MUD architecture and our system is structural, not metaphorical: the same data structures, the same separation of concerns, the same lifecycle patterns.

The Driver/Mudlib Separation

The deepest architectural pattern in LPMud is the clean split between the driver (a C/C++ runtime that provides primitives) and the mudlib (an LPC library that builds the world from those primitives). FluffOS defines its primitive interface through .spec files in src/packages/:

item.title: MCP and Claude Agent SDK: Deep Dive
item.description:
This text examines the Model Context Protocol (MCP) specification (version 2025-11-25) and Anthropic’s Claude Agent SDK to identify capabilities we are not using, patterns for multi-agent orchestration, and concrete opportunities to enrich our 561-datastore MCP server and agentic harness.

Part 1: The Model Context Protocol

MCP is a JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol connecting hosts (LLM applications), clients (connectors within the host), and servers (services providing context). The architecture enforces isolation: each client-server pair is a stateful session, and servers never see the full conversation or each other’s state. The host mediates everything.

item.title: Model Training as Algebraic Closure
item.description: A closure operator on a set is a function cl that is extensive (S is a subset of cl(S)), monotone (if S is a subset of T then cl(S) is a subset of cl(T)), and idempotent (cl(cl(S)) = cl(S)). The elements of cl(S) that were not in S are the consequences generated by closing over S. A fixed point of cl is any set F where cl(F) = F — closure adds nothing new.

item.title: Multi-agent orchestration frameworks 2026
item.description:
Three frameworks have emerged as dominant platforms for building multi-agent AI systems as of 2026: CrewAI, AutoGen 0.4, and LangGraph. Each represents a different design philosophy and targets different use cases. Understanding their core differences is essential for choosing the right orchestration layer for a given problem.

Framework Overview

CrewAI

Architecture: Python-only, high-level, role-playing oriented. Independent of LangChain, built entirely from scratch as a lean, lightning-fast framework.

Core metaphor: A crew of specialists, each with a role, goal, backstory, and set of tools. Tasks flow through the crew sequentially or hierarchically, with agents executing against LLM-backed personas.

item.title: Non-LLM Computation for Agent Duties
item.description:
Language model inference is expensive, slow, and non-deterministic. For structured agent work — routing tasks to offices, selecting tools, updating records — it is also unnecessary. This text examines what computation can replace LLM inference for agent duties, grounded in published research and empirical tool-call data from this system.

The Claim

A significant fraction of what agent systems use language models for — tool selection, task routing, status updates, record-keeping — involves decisions that are computable given the inputs. These decisions do not require language generation. They require classification, lookup, or structured prediction. Using an LLM for them is like using a search engine to add two numbers.

item.title: OpenClaw Architecture Analysis
item.description: OpenClaw is an open-source personal AI assistant that runs on user devices and answers through messaging channels (WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, iMessage, Matrix, and more). Unlike coding-focused harnesses such as Claude Code or Cline, OpenClaw targets the “always-on personal assistant” use case — a gateway that manages channels, tools, memory, and proactive behaviors across a persistent agent lifecycle. The source is TypeScript (ESM), Node 22+, with a plugin architecture that makes most capabilities optional.

item.title: OpenHands Architecture Analysis
item.description:
OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin) is an open-source AI software development platform. This analysis covers the V0 architecture (deprecated April 2026, with V1 migrating to a separate Software Agent SDK). The patterns here remain instructive regardless of version.

Runtime Isolation

OpenHands isolates agent execution inside Docker containers. Each agent session gets a dedicated container (openhands-runtime-{session_id}) with its own filesystem, network, and process namespace. The container runs as a dedicated non-root user (openhands, UID 42420) to prevent UID conflicts with the host.

item.title: Reflexion and LATS — Verbal Reinforcement and Tree Search for Language Agents
item.description:
Two systems from the same Princeton research lineage (Shinn, Yao, Narasimhan) that solve the same problem differently: how does a language agent learn from failure without gradient updates?

Reflexion: failure becomes language becomes behavior change

The core loop

Reflexion implements a three-component cycle — actor, evaluator, self-reflection — that runs across trials (not within a single episode). The agent attempts a task, fails, reflects on why it failed in natural language, then uses that reflection as additional context on the next attempt.

item.title: Small Model Fine-Tuning for Agentic Tool Use, April 2026
item.description:
Claim

As of April 2026, fine-tuning small open-source models (1B-8B parameters) for tool use and function calling is practical, cheap, and produces models that rival frontier systems on structured benchmarks. The gap between a $0.50/hour rented GPU running QLoRA and a GPT-4o API call has narrowed to the point where specialized small models outperform general large models on function-calling tasks.

The Landscape: What Changed

Two years ago, tool use required frontier models. Today, Salesforce’s xLAM-2-3b-fc-r — a 3-billion parameter model — tops the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard, outperforming GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet on multi-turn function calling. This is not an anomaly. The convergence of three developments made it possible:

item.title: SWE-agent Architecture Analysis
item.description:
SWE-agent is an autonomous software engineering agent from Princeton NLP that solves GitHub issues by operating inside a containerized environment with a carefully designed set of tools. Its core contribution is the Agent-Computer Interface (ACI) — the principle that how you expose the environment to an agent matters as much as the model itself. This analysis covers the architecture from the source at commit depth 1 of princeton-nlp/SWE-agent.

1. Agent-Computer Interface (ACI)

The ACI is SWE-agent’s central design idea: rather than giving the agent a raw shell, you give it a curated set of commands that constrain the action space to operations the agent can reason about reliably.

item.title: The Personal AI OS Landscape in 2026
item.description:
By early 2026, the idea that an AI should be a persistent, stateful operating system rather than a stateless query-response tool has moved from research speculation to shipped products. This survey maps the landscape: who is building what, how they handle the core problems of persistence, learning, and multi-agent coordination, and what architectural patterns have emerged.

The field divides into five categories: hardware-native AI operating systems, memory infrastructure layers, multi-agent orchestration frameworks, autonomous coding agents, and personal agent platforms. Each solves a different slice of the problem. None yet solves all of it.

item.title: Voyager Skill Library Analysis
item.description:
Voyager is an LLM-powered agent for Minecraft that learns an open-ended repertoire of skills without human intervention. It demonstrates three mechanisms directly relevant to our harness: a semantic skill library, a self-verification critic, and an automatic curriculum. This analysis is based on the source code at MineDojo/Voyager (commit as of 2026-04-04).

Architecture Overview

Voyager’s main loop (voyager.py:learn) runs indefinitely:

  1. Curriculum agent proposes the next task based on the agent’s current state, completed tasks, and failed tasks.
  2. Action agent generates JavaScript code to execute the task in Minecraft, receiving relevant skills from the library as context.
  3. Critic agent inspects the post-execution game state and judges whether the task succeeded, providing critique if it failed.
  4. On success, the skill manager extracts the working code, generates a natural-language description, and stores both in the skill library.
  5. On failure, the curriculum agent records the failure and proposes a different task.

The loop has no terminal condition except an iteration cap. This is true open-ended learning — there is no predefined goal list.
